## Bug Report

Please answer these questions before submitting your issue. Thanks!

### 1. Minimal reproduce step (Required)

```
> create table t(id varchar(32) primary key, i int, v int, key i(i));
> insert into t values('a', 1, 1), ('b', 2, 2), ('c', 3, 3);
> select /*+ index_lookup_pushdown(t, i) */ * from t where id != 'a' order by id limit 1;
```

### 2. What did you expect to see? (Required)

```
> select /*+ index_lookup_pushdown(t, i) */ * from t where id != 'a' order by id limit 1;
+----+---+---+
| id | i | v |
+----+---+---+
| b  | 2 | 2 |
+----+---+---+
```

### 3. What did you see instead (Required)

```
> select /*+ index_lookup_pushdown(t, i) */ * from t where id != 'a' order by id limit 1;
+----+---+---+
| id | i | v |
+----+---+---+
| a  | 1 | 1 |
+----+---+---+
```

### 4. What is your TiDB version? (Required)

master

## Bug Report

<!-- Thanks for your bug report! Don't worry if you can't fill out all the sections. -->

### What version of TiKV are you using?

v4.0.11
### Steps to reproduce

Pessimistic transactions may contain keys that don't need pessimistic locks. These locks are prewritten without any contraint checks.

So, there is a possible case. After a pessimistic transaction is committed, a non-pessimistic lock of the same transaction can  be also prewritten successfully. The lock TS of such a lock is smaller than the commit TS of the latest write record.

This breaks the assumption of the `DeltaScanner`. When a lock is encountered, it needs to find latest write record as the old value. It asserts that the commit TS of the record must be smaller than the lock TS. https://github.com/tikv/tikv/blob/v4.0.11/src/storage/mvcc/reader/scanner/mod.rs#L389-L392

This is probably the cause of the panic below.

CDC can still work in this case.

tikv/tikv#12796.body:
## Development Task

It's always arduous to update grafana template as the grid position of json may change vastly everytime of adding just a new panel.

We can leverage [grafonnet](https://grafana.github.io/grafonnet-lib/) to generate the json template from code (here is a talk keynote
https://inuits.eu/pdfs/Grafana_as_Code.pdf). Then we don't need to struggle with the json changes, just to add a new query in code, and the grid position will be taken care of by the generator. The code would be like this, the noise of json is avoided and easy to reorganize and reuse same panel in different dashboards:

```jsonnet
local halfGridPos = {
    x: 0,
    y: 0,
    w: 12,
    h: 8,
}; // occupies half of a row

dashboard.new(
    title='Test-Cluster-TiKV-Details',
    schemaVersion=27,
)
.addRow(
    row.new('Cluster')
        .addPanel(
            graphPanel.new('The storage size per TiKV instance')
                .addTarget('sum(tikv_store_size_bytes{k8s_cluster="$k8s_cluster", tidb_cluster="$tidb_cluster", instance=~"$instance", type="used"}) by (instance)'),
            halfGridPos
        )
        .addPanel(
            graphPanel.new('Approximate Region size')
                .addTarget('sum(tikv_raftstore_region_size_sum{k8s_cluster="$k8s_cluster", tidb_cluster="$tidb_cluster", instance=~"$instance"}) by (instance)'),
            halfGridPos
        )
        .addPanel(..., halfGridPos)
)
.addRow(
    row.new(title='Errors')
        .addPanel(..., halfGridPos)
)
.addRow(
    row.new(title='Server')
        .addPanel(..., halfGridPos)
)
```

tikv/tikv#17221.body:
## Feature Request
I do see that tikv-server handles SIGTERM to properly shutdown itself however, I see one major issue is that it doesn't inform the PD server that it is exiting before shutting down, as a result, what happens is that when the new queries are fired to TiDB server they still don't know this server is dead and they end up trying to connect with the dead TiKV pod, and after a timeout, they realize it's dead and then PD eventually understand that and start leader eviction from the dead TiKV pod. All this happens within a few seconds but still, queries during this small period get affected and latency increases. What I would rather want is that tikv-server itself informs PD during a graceful shutdown to add an evict-leader scheduler so that PD removes all the leaders before the pod is dead.

### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe:
<!-- A description of what the problem is. -->
This issue comes up when there is a centrally managed k8s cluster that holds not only TiDB clusters and TiKV uses local attached disks. When they start k8s API server upgrades the nodes go down for 5m, PD is unaware of this downtime and until it figures out the pod is down it continues to send queries to dead TiKV. Which results in latency spikes for ~5s duration. 

### Describe the feature you'd like:
<!-- A description of what you want to happen. -->
When kubectl drain is run on the node, it sends SIGTERM to the TiKV pod, I would like the tikv-server informs PD that it is going down and remove the leaders before going down

### Describe alternatives you've considered:
<!-- A description of any alternative solutions or features you've considered. -->
We have already gone through https://docs.pingcap.com/tidb-in-kubernetes/stable/maintain-a-kubernetes-node#if-the-node-storage-can-be-automatically-migrated-1 but this requires manual intervention before node is going down, which is not possible as the k8s clusters are centrally managed.

### Teachability, Documentation, Adoption, Migration Strategy:
<!-- If you can, explain some scenarios how users might use this, or situations in which it would be helpful. Any API designs, mockups, or diagrams are also helpful. -->
tikv/tikv#17221.title: Graceful Shutdown of TiKV pods when SIGTERM is sent to the pod

tikv/tikv#18124.body:
## Feature Request

### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe:
During log restore operations, if a TiKV node experiences slow downloads (e.g., due to network throttling or transient bandwidth issues), it can cause significant performance degradation across the cluster. This includes prolonged log apply durations, increased CPU and memory usage on the affected node, and even restore process failures. Currently, there is no mechanism for TiKV to reject requests from the BR client when it is under such stress.

### Describe the feature you'd like:
<!-- A description of what you want to happen. -->
Implement a flow control mechanism on TiKV during log restore to reject or delay requests from the BR client if the node detects slow download performance or is approaching resource limits. The flow control mechanism should:
1. Monitor key metrics, such as download latency, in-flight RPC requests, CPU usage, and memory consumption.
2. Dynamically rate-limit or reject incoming requests from the BR client if the node detects:
    - Sustained slow downloads.
    - Resource contention (e.g., excessive CPU or memory usage).
3. Provide detailed feedback to the BR client, allowing it to adjust its restore rate or redistribute workloads to other nodes.

### Describe alternatives you've considered:
<!-- A description of any alternative solutions or features you've considered. -->

### Teachability, Documentation, Adoption, Migration Strategy:
<!-- If you can, explain some scenarios how users might use this, or situations in which it would be helpful. Any API designs, mockups, or diagrams are also helpful. -->
tikv/tikv#18124.title: Log Restore: Add Flow Control mechanism

tikv/tikv#18169.body:
## Bug Report

<!-- Thanks for your bug report! Don't worry if you can't fill out all the sections. -->


1. Our production CDC instance running in Kubernetes was restarted.  
2. After the restart, the memory usage of TiKV-CDC began to increase continuously.  
3. Eventually, the memory quota (512 MB) was fully utilized, causing the Changefeed to hang.  
4. Restarting the CDC instance multiple times did not resolve the issue. The memory quota of TiKV-CDC remained at 512 MB, and the Changefeed remained stuck.


### What version of TiKV are you using?
<!-- You can run `tikv-server --version` -->

- 14 TiKV nodes with v7.5.4
- 2 TiCDC nodes with v7.5.4

### What operating system and CPU are you using?
<!-- If you're using Linux, you can run `cat /proc/cpuinfo` -->

### Steps to reproduce
<!-- If possible, provide a recipe for reproducing the error. A complete runnable program is good. -->

### What did you expect?


- TiKV-CDC should release memory after the restart and operate within the configured memory quota.  
- The Changefeed should resume normal processing after restarting the CDC instance.


### What did happened?

- TiKV-CDC's memory usage continued to increase after the restart and eventually hit the 512 MB quota.  
- The Changefeed was stuck and unable to recover.  
- Restarting the CDC instance multiple times did not free up memory or resolve the issue. The memory quota of TiKV-CDC remained at 512 MB.

As a result, the server reported the "Server is busy" error and prevented PD from transferring leaders to the TiKV instance. ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/99729846-c58c-4851-a34e-16c217e58db6) During this time, Raftstore had no operations, indicating that the peers were not performing any actions. ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9a158712-b8e2-490b-ac7c-f1846066d590) One potential cause is that the peers went into hibernation before successfully exiting the "busy apply" state. ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c0c7ff79-5db1-4f3f-bd47-cc30f836ad74) ### What version of TiKV are you using? v8.5.1

tikv/tikv#18243.body:
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a9f47366-4e54-4c08-8d93-4d9f1d89db23)

### Background
A region becomes leader while a new log backup task is initialized.

### Reproduction Timeline
0. The log backup endpoint register ranges of the new log backup task to the observers.
1/2. The region becomes pre-candidate, which trigger to send `Task::ModifyObserve(Observe::Stop)` to the `Endpoint::scheduler`.
3/4. The region becomes candidate, which trigger to send `Task::ModifyObserve(Observe::Stop)` to the `Endpoint::scheduler`.
5/6. The region becomes leader, which trigger to send `Task::ModifyObserve(Observe::Start{ObserveId: 191299})` to the `Endpoint::scheduler`.

After the step 6, the `Endpoint::scheduler` has the followed messages that have not been consumed.
```
Task::ModifyObserve(Observe::Stop)
Task::ModifyObserve(Observe::Stop)
Task::ModifyObserve(Observe::Start{ObserveId: 191299})
```

7. The initialization of new log backup scan the region and send `Observe::Start{ObserveId:191374}` to the `Observer::scheduler`.

After the step 7, the `Observer::scheduler` has the followed messages that have not been consumed.
```
Observe::Start{ObserveId:191374}
```

8. The `Endpoint` starts to consumes the above messages in the scheduler, and sends them to `Observer::scheduler`.

After the step 8, the `Observer::scheduler` has the followed messages that have not been consumed.
```
Observe::Start{ObserveId:191374}
Observe::Stop
Observe::Stop
Observe::Start{ObserveId: 191299}
```

This order causes the region to not be observed with the error `stale observe id ObserveId(191299), current id: ObserveId(191374)`.
tikv/tikv#18243.title: The log backup observer loses observation of a region

tikv/tikv#18262.body:
## Development Task
Caller may just want to make sure the store is recently flushed. But if force flush fails when encountering running flush, the client then has no idea about when this flush will finish.

An example is when doing rolling restart, we want every store flushed before it restarted. Hence a `FlushNow` wan sent, but then when it responses "another flush is running" we can do few things but back off and retry; it will be better to wait the running flush and return "success" after the running flush finished.
tikv/tikv#18262.title: log_backup: force flush should wait until flush done

The current implementation of raftstore threads directly interacts with RaftEngine and RocksDB during split, merge, and configuration changes. tikv/tikv#18359.body: ## Bug Report ### What version of TiKV are you using? v6.5.2 ### What happened? It was observed on a TiKV cluster that memory usage increased by 30 GB within 10 minutes, eventually leading to an OOM. During this period, the resolved-ts worker was stuck at 100% CPU. ### Analysis Raftstore continuously sends ChangeLog entries (including committed writes to the lock cf and write cf) to the resolved-ts worker. When the worker is stuck, tasks will accumulate. There is currently no mechanism to bound the memory consumption of the accumulated tasks. Monitoring shows that the memory usage curve closely tracks the growth of pending resolved-ts tasks. The Pending Command Size metric reached 7 GB, although it may underestimate the actual memory usage. ### TODO - Reproduce this on a local cluster by simulating a blocked resolved-ts worker. - Design a mechanism to bound the memory usage of accumulated tasks for the resolved-ts worker.
tikv/tikv#18359.title: TiKV OOM: resolved-ts worker stuck at 100% CPU with growing memory from pending command size

tikv/tikv#18451.body:
## Bug Report

similar with https://github.com/tikv/tikv/issues/18403, if there are lots pending tasks in cleanup_worker and lots regions need to be compacted, 

TiKV may find there are some large regions that can not be split,
that is, all the mvcc versions accumulated in these regions are all belongs to a same row, in this case, TiKV will init a Task::Compact task to the `cleanup_worker`

https://github.com/tikv/tikv/blob/0a97ca8ab18cdbcb626ee4e24b6db80ad472fd71/components/raftstore/src/store/peer.rs#L4562-L4588 

and for `cleanup_worker`,  if it is busy processing pending tasks, the duplicated Task::Compact maybe send to `cleanup_worker` again and again(interval 10s) until the first `Task::Compact` finished and clear the redundant old mvcc versions.

``` 
Mar 21, 2025 @ 08:03:14.277 | tikv | error | [split_observer.rs:148] ["failed to handle split req"] [err="\"no valid key found for split.\""] [region_id=157887931] [thread_id=106]

Mar 21, 2025 @ 08:03:24.268 | tikv | error | [split_observer.rs:148] ["failed to handle split req"] [err="\"no valid key found for split.\""] [region_id=157887931] [thread_id=106]

Mar 21, 2025 @ 08:03:34.277 [split_observer.rs:148] ["failed to handle split req"] [err="\"no valid key found for split.\""] [region_id=157887931] [thread_id=105]
```

At this time, these duplicate `Task::Compact` will still be in the pending tasks of `cleanup_worker`, and when it is its turn, because we do not check the redundant mvccs here, we directly send the manual compact task to rocksdb.

https://github.com/tikv/tikv/blob/0a97ca8ab18cdbcb626ee4e24b6db80ad472fd71/components/raftstore/src/store/worker/compact.rs#L380-L409 

However, in fact, the duplicate manual::compact task can no longer do anything at this time, but it will take up the time of the `cleanup-worker` to handle and wait the compaction job finished, thereby blocking the execution of the other tasks that do need compaction.

### What version of TiKV are you using?
master


### Steps to reproduce
A big cluster and enable TTL, meanwhile, update some keys frequently

### What did you expect?

big region that belongs to one row could be compaction in time
tikv/tikv#18451.title: Task::Compact may result in a large number of repeated and useless manual::compact tasks

tikv/tikv#18463.body:
## Development Task

This issue originates from customer requirements following the previous enhancement in [#17884](https://github.com/tikv/tikv/issues/17884), which aimed to improve TiKV’s SLA during I/O hangs on KVDB (when deployed with separate mount paths).

Recent customer feedback highlighted the need to extend detection coverage to I/O jitters, ensuring TiKV can automatically recover from abnormal states caused by KVDB I/O jitters.

Therefore, this ticket is built to tracks the development efforts to enhance TiKV’s I/O jitter detection and recovery mechanism.
tikv/tikv#18463.title: HA: inspect the I/O jitters on kvdb if using separate mount paths.

tikv/tikv#18464.body:
## Feature Request

Read pool thread is configured to be very large number (5x of CPU) because our average EBS latency are in the range of 1ms.  However, this large read pool sometimes leads to CPU bottlenecks in TIKV when there are hot regions. As a result, the blast radius increases, and the cluster experiences extended recovery times since other threads, such as raft and resolved-ts threads, become slower.
Given the high EBS latency and the use of RocksDB in synchronous mode, we recommend making the system more adaptive by implementing the following changes:
1. Limit the CPU utilization of the unified read pool thread through a configuration parameter.
2. Currently, the unified read pool scales up or down based solely on the CPU utilization of each thread. It should also consider the wait time of tasks to determine when to adjust its size.

This solution is also useful to enable resource control group(RCG). RCG fair scheduling in TiKV doesn't work, impacting all the tenants if TiKV CPU becomes a bottleneck

tikv/tikv#18600.body:
## Bug Report

### What version of TiKV are you using?
nightly

### Steps to reproduce
<!-- If possible, provide a recipe for reproducing the error. A complete runnable program is good. -->

Write highly compressible 500KB values to TiKV with Titan enabled:
```
def compressible_str(length):
    base = ''.join(random.choices("abcde", k=8))  # small alphabet
    return (base * (length // len(base) + 1))[:length]
```

### What did you expect?
Regions should split once they reach the configured size threshold.

### What did happened?
No region split occurred. Even though actual data size grew significantly, the approximate_region_size remained low.

### Cause
When estimating the size of a region that includes Titan blobs, TiKV uses the compressed size of the blobs recorded in the blob index. As a result, under high compression ratios, the estimated region size can be significantly smaller than the actual data size, leading to delayed region splits and large regions.

### Proposed Fix
Add a `raw_size` field to BlobIndex, recording the uncompressed size of each blob. During region size estimation, TiKV should use `raw_size` instead of compressed blob size. Note: We need to ensure data format compatibility for existing blob indexes.
tikv/tikv#18600.title: Region too large due to size underestimation with Titan on highly compressible data

tikv/tikv#18695.body:
Summary

The current GC mechanism triggers compaction on a per-region basis when MVCC stats exceed a threshold. This can lead to inefficient GC behavior, especially when many regions accumulate MVCC garbage unevenly.

Proposed Improvement

Instead of checking each region independently, periodically:
- Collect MVCC stats from all regions.
- Rank regions by a GC score (e.g., based on MVCC tombstone volume, version count, etc.).
- Compact only the top N highest-ranked regions per GC round.

Motivation

- Avoid long GC rounds caused by evaluating and compacting too many regions.
- Prevent GC starvation in “hot” regions that generate MVCC data faster than GC cycles can keep up.
- Make GC effort more focused and cost-effective by prioritizing the worst regions.
tikv/tikv#18695.title: Score and Rank Regions by MVCC Stats for Prioritized Compaction

tikv/tikv#18708.body: When flow control is enabled: - If `level0-slowdown-writes-trigger` and `level0-stop-writes-trigger` are explicitly set, `l0-file-threshold` overrides them **only when it is smaller**. --> This behavior is likely intend for upgrade compatiability. Refer to this [comment](https://github.com/tikv/tikv/pull/12127#discussion_r834126610). - If they are not set, it overrides them unconditionally. --> This is required for correctness on SST ingestion flow control. In current implementation `ingest_maybe_stall` relies on the `level0-slowdown-writes-trigger` but the actual slowdown is controled by the `flow-control.l0-file-threshold`. So the simplest way to ensure `ingest_maybe_stall` integrates well with the flow control is to keep this two configuation in sync. Refer to https://github.com/tikv/tikv/pull/11840. Similarly, `flow-control.soft-pending-compaction-bytes-limit` also overrides `rocksdb.*cf.soft-pending-compaction-bytes-limit`, and `rocksdb.*cf.hard-pending-compaction-bytes-limit` also overrides `rocksdb.*cf.hard-pending-compaction-bytes-limit`. In recent tests, we encountered performance instability when flow control was enabled and either `flow-contrl.l0-file-threshold` or `flow-control.soft-pending-compaction-bytes-limit` was increased. Although the intention was to reduce the frequency of triggering flow control for stable performance, in reality it became more frequent, and the performance became less stable. As shown in the figure below: Upon investgation, we found the root cause: compaction QPS dropped after increase this configurations. This is because RocksDB speeds up compaction when approaching `level0-slowdown-writes-trigger` and `soft-pending-compaction-bytes-limit`. Increase these values wakens the compaction spped up mechanism. To address this issue, I think we should not overriding the `level0-slowdown-writes-trigger` and `soft-pending-compaction-bytes-limit`, and `ingest_maybe_stall` can reply on `level0-stop-writes-trigger`, which can still be override by the `l0-file-threshold`, preserving correctness.
tikv/tikv#18708.title: Decouple l0-file-threshold and level0-slowdown-writes-trigger

tikv/tikv#18727.body:
Why?
- Architectural Benefits
  Moving this functionality to gc_worker provides better architectural alignment since it's inherently GC-oriented, making it the natural home for this logic.
- Configuration Access Challenges
  The enable_compaction_filter setting directly affects compaction score calculations but is only accessible within the gc_worker module. While it supports online configuration, passing this parameter through to raftstore would require extensive and inelegant code changes throughout the system.
- Enhanced Scheduling Strategy
  Following discussions with @Connor1996, we want to fully leverage the check interval mechanism. For example, with a 5-minute check cycle, we should process compaction candidates in priority order until the time limit is reached, then re-evaluate since statistics may have changed. The current raftstore worker uses an action model pattern, which would require significant modifications to support self-referencing for deadline management. In contrast, gc_worker can implement this more elegantly with a dedicated thread running in a loop - if approaching the deadline, it moves to the next iteration; if completing early, it simply sleeps.
- Dependency Reduction
  As discussed in our weekly meeting, we aim to reduce reliance on raftstore.meta, which requires lock guard access and can impact tail latency for command processing. Since we're already considering a refactor, it makes sense to implement it in the optimal location from the start.
tikv/tikv#18727.title: Move TiKV GC oriented compaction to GC module

tikv/tikv#18797.body:
## Development Task

ref https://github.com/tikv/pd/issues/9359

Assume a store in the cluster is experiencing network jitter. This jitter causes region leaders in the store to be re-campaigned by raft on other stores, resulting in leader transfers. However, PD is unable to detect the network jitter on the TiKV store, because PD can recieve the store heartbeat in this network status. Consequently, the PD's balance leader scheduler schedules the leader back to the problematic TiKV, leading to increased latency in the cluster.

To address the above problem, we need to add a new network status feedback mechanism to PD, and avoid to transfer leaders to the problematic TiKV node.

- [x] Establish a network detection mechanism between tikv <-> tikv
- [x] Calculate the slow score based on the network delay and upload it to PD
tikv/tikv#18797.title: Avoid the re-balance during network jitter

## Development Task
Currently, the internal TopSQL system of TiKV has two types of recorders, cpuRecorder and summaryRecorder, and the main Data Acquisition process is as follows:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/60d7a505-4d49-4e61-b69d-1d14f33a021a)

The data collected by cpuRecorder and summaryRecorder will be stored in global RawRecords, then sorted by cpu usage time, the top N data will be selected, and the remaining data will be merged into "other". tikv/tikv#18838.body:
## Feature Request

### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe:
<!-- A description of what the problem is. -->
The Raw KV API has `CompareAndSwap` which allows us to atomically read-modify-write a key. This is a very helpful API for users of the RAW KV API who don't want to incur the cost of using the Txn API. However, it becomes difficult to safely use this API to delete a key without an accompanying atomic "compare-and-delete" operation. 

Here's an example, imagine that `key=foo` is already stored in the KV store:
1. [thread1] Reads `key=foo`, evaluates some condition, and decides to delete the key. It issues an atomic `Delete` operation
2. [thread1] The `Delete` operation is running slow (network blip, slow server, whatever)
3. [thread2] Does an atomic `CompareAndSwap` operation to update the key
4. [thread2] The `CompareAndSwap` succeeds and the new value is stored
5. [thread1] The slow running `Delete` operation eventually completes and removes the key

The update we did in step4 has now been erased! If we had an atomic `CompareAndDelete` operation then we'd be able to mitigate this scenario. The deleting thread would issue a `CompareAndDelete` to ensure the value is only deleted if another update does not happen in the meantime.

### Describe the feature you'd like:
<!-- A description of what you want to happen. -->
There are two options here:
1. Extend the current CompareAndSwap API to support key deletion. This could be done by extending the `RawCASRequest` protobuf message to include a flag which specifies that we want to delete the key if it matches the expected value, or
2. Introduce a whole new CompareAndDelete API.

Note: we tried a very rough PoC implementation here: https://github.com/tikv/tikv/compare/master...Mojachieee:tikv:compareAndDeleteHack?expand=1. The implementation hijacks the current compare and swap code path and treats `new_value.empty()` as a request to delete the key. This is obviously broken as TiKV allows writing empty values. Aside from this hack, we'd be interested to know whether or not that patch is all that is needed to implement CompareAndDelete? 

### Describe alternatives you've considered:
<!-- A description of any alternative solutions or features you've considered. -->
A work-around is to use CompareAndSwap to set an application specific "tombstone" value. This signals to the application that the key is semantically deleted. However, you're then left with the problem of how do you safely remove the tombstone. If we are sure the key will never be reused then there's no problem we can just remove the key. If there's any possibility that the key can be reused (i.e. Put again) then a atomic Delete is not sufficient. It can race with a contending atomic CompareAndSwap,

### Teachability, Documentation, Adoption, Migration Strategy:
<!-- If you can, explain some scenarios how users might use this, or situations in which it would be helpful. Any API designs, mockups, or diagrams are also helpful. -->
tikv/tikv#18838.title: Implement CompareAndDelete for the RawKV API

tikv/tikv#18844.body:
## Development Task
Currently, RawRecords are aggregated by "sql+plan+table+region+key_range". We pick the topN RawRecords ordered by cpu time. Other RawRecords which are not in topN are merged into one single "Other" record. Since "N" is a limit number, when queries access many different regions, the length of RawRecords will be much larger than N, and many records which map to one of the topN "sql+plan" will be merged into the "Other" record. It would cause data distortion in some cases.  
For example:
A cluster with only 1 tikv node, running QueryA and QueryB
QueryA access 100 regions concurrently in one second, each takes 100.5ms tikv cpu time.
QueryB access 100 regions concurrently in one second, takes [150, 149, 148,... 51]ms time.
We will get 200 RawRecords for these 200 region requests, and then 49 of QueryA and 50 of QueryB will be picked, others will be merged. So in the final picked records, we would conclude that QueryB takes much larger cpu time than QueryA. However, they actually take almost the same cpu time.

So we can aggregate RawRecords using "sql+plan+table" before picking topN records to reduce such data distortion cases.
tikv/tikv#18844.title: Aggregate RawRecords before picking topN records

tikv/tikv#18846.body:
## Bug Report

<!-- Thanks for your bug report! Don't worry if you can't fill out all the sections. -->

### What version of TiKV are you using?

v8.5.3, v8.5.2

### What operating system and CPU are you using?
<!-- If you're using Linux, you can run `cat /proc/cpuinfo` -->

### Steps to reproduce

BR restore on a cluster of 20 TiKVs with data source being a Huawei OBS (an S3-compatible service). 

The upstream service implemented rate limiting through a token-bucket filter, forcing the total download speed from the entire TiKV cluster to be below 1 GiB/s.

The Rust AWS SDK has a feature known as "stalled stream protection" awslabs/aws-sdk-rust#1146, which will kill the request if the throughput is below 1 B/s for some grace period (supposed to be 20s, but it seems the actual timeout is 6s).

So if the total downstream speed is too high, which exhausted the token bucket, and the penalty exceeded the grace period (6s), the AWS SDK will kill the connection, forcing TiKV to restart the downstream from the beginning, both worsening the resource usage and leads to #18839.

### What did you expect?

Speed limit should not kill the download request (at least not within 6s).

### What did happened?

The download request failed with "streaming error".
tikv/tikv#18846.title: BR restore / S3: Stalled stream protection interferes with QoS-based rate limiting

tikv/tikv#18872.body: ## Development Task For now, the calculation logic of cache compact idx works as follows: When a node's `replicated_idx` is less than the leader's `truncated_idx`, the `alive_cache_idx` is not adjusted. In scenarios where a node becomes isolated (e.g., due to network partition or failure) and the leader performs a forced log GC due to log accumulation, the `truncated_idx `may become greater than or equal to the isolated node's `replicated_idx`. This leads to the `alive_cache_idx` ignoring the lagging node's sync progress. Since the leader determines whether a snapshot is outdated based on: `min(alive_cache_idx, apply_idx+1) > snapshot.index` the snapshot generation will be canceled if this condition is met. Under continuous writes, both `alive_cache_idx` and `apply_idx` keep increasing beyond the snapshot index, causing the snapshot to never be generated, preventing the lagging node from ever catching up. ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d63425f7-2f6d-404b-89ff-150750619606)
tikv/tikv#18872.title: Unreasonable snapshot cancel checking

tikv/tikv#18939.body:
## Bug Report

<!-- Thanks for your bug report! Don't worry if you can't fill out all the sections. -->

In one cluster we saw the read request from the default resource group (priority is medium) running very slow while all the tikv instance did not meet any resource bottleneck.

From the metrics, I saw the overall cpu usage is around 500%(with 8cores) and the unified read pool cpu usage is under 100% while the unified read pool cpu running tasks are high.  But most tikv instances' medium and low priority limiter's quota is set to 500ms which is the default min value.

**Analysis**: The current priority limit auto-tune logic is based on the proportion of readpool(unified read pool and scheduler worker pool) cpu usage and the process total cpu usage. It use this proportion to calculate the total cpu quota for all priority. But this logic can only be correct if the workload from all resource groups are the same pattern. In this case, because most cpu usage is from write so the total quota for priority limiter is limited to a very low value that is not fit for heavy read workload.
tikv/tikv#18939.title: resource_control: slow read caused by auto priority limiter

tikv/tikv#18949.body:

To guarantee the consistency for IndexLookUp read, we need also check the memory locks in `ExtraSnapStoreAccessor` when `get_local_region_storage` is called.

## Bug Report

In some production environments, the total sst files count is more than 200k and the manifest file after compact is bigger than 128MiB. This trigger a compact on every change of the file.

We should exlarge the default manifest size to avoid the unnecessary compact.

tikv/tikv#19006.body:
Today things like blob store access on azure is controlled by authentication via fixed azure keys/secrets as mentioned in: [Grant Permissions to Remote Storage](https://docs.pingcap.com/tidb-in-kubernetes/stable/grant-permissions-to-remote-storage#azure-account-permissions)

This approach is less secure than managed identities which is managed and provisioned by azure gated by the vm identity and we would like to switch to using that: [Managed identities for Azure resources - Managed identities for Azure resources](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/managed-identities-azure-resources/overview)

Currently this not appear to be supported and we would like to make a feature request to have this supported.

Notably instead of using a k8s secret (that is fixed and requires us to manage/rotate), I believe this feature would require binaries (like tikv) to query the local endpoint to obtain the credentials

e.g. [How managed identities for Azure resources work with Azure virtual machines - Managed identities for Azure resources](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/managed-identities-azure-resources/how-managed-identities-work-vm)

curl 'http://169.254.169.254/metadata/identity/oauth2/token?api-version=2018-02-01&resource=https%3A%2F%2Fstorage.azure.com%2F' -H Metadata:true
tikv/tikv#19006.title: BR: make br support managed identity for Azure

## Bug Report

<!-- Thanks for your bug report! Don't worry if you can't fill out all the sections. -->

### What version of TiKV are you using?
Current Master.

### What operating system and CPU are you using?
N/A

### Steps to reproduce
1. Start a `tikv-ctl compact-log-backup`.
2. Abort it by `ctrl-c`, then restart it from checkpoint.
3. Restart `tikv-ctl compact-log-backup` from checkpoint.

### What did you expect?
All compacted log file spans are included in the `migration`. Just like the compaction was finished within oneshot.

### What did happened?
The `migration` it generated contains deletion to log files in step `3` only.

tikv/tikv#19070.body: ## Development Task When a TiKV peer becomes unavailable, region leaders on other nodes are prevented from entering hibernation due to the following logic: https://github.com/tikv/tikv/blob/d50767d80798053594eb5bd3ccf01ee3fc79f462/components/raftstore/src/store/fsm/peer.rs#L2487-L2495 This forces all leaders to remain active, causing a massive surge in Raft heartbeat traffic. In a cluster with a high number of Regions, this surge consumes excessive CPU in the Raftstore, potentially leading to CPU exhaustion and performance degradation. One typical scenario is described below: There is a 3-tikv instance cluster, and each tikv has 30w regions on it. Heartbeat message before down one tikv: heartbeat message sent after down one tikv: ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0c785210-cea4-4cbf-8c37-da543ddb84ea) Raftstore CPU usage before down one tikv. Raftstore CPU usage after down one tikv:
tikv/tikv#19070.title: Raftstore CPU Exhaustion: leaders prevented from hibernating after down one tikv instance

tikv/tikv#19133.body:
## Feature Request
TiKV may skip or deprioritize compaction when the usual compaction thresholds are not reached, or when certain regions contain a larger-than-average number of MVCC versions.
However, some of these regions still experience heavy read traffic and end up scanning far more MVCC versions than low-traffic regions with an equal or greater number of MVCC versions.
This feature adjusts the compaction score calculation to factor in the number of MVCC versions actually read during query traffic, ensuring that frequently accessed regions are compacted in a timely manner.

tikv/tikv#19179.body:
Coprocessor (`copr`) tasks may experience long **total suspend time**, which represents periods when a cop task is not actively being processed. Today, this information is only available in TiKV’s slow-query logs, making it difficult to diagnose copr latency problems from metrics alone.

Suspend time can be caused by multiple factors, including:

- Yatp scheduling delay  
- Waiting for the copr concurrency-limiter semaphore  

Semaphore waiting, in particular, is a common contributor. The concurrency limiter is designed to prioritize completing a limited number of heavy tasks instead of allowing too many tasks to run concurrently and all make slow progress. When a cop task runs for more than 5 ms, it must acquire a semaphore permit before continuing, and the number of permits defaults to the number of CPU cores.

To improve observability, we should expose:
1. Total suspend time as a metric (not just in slow-query logs)  
2. Semaphore waiting time per task  
3. Number of cop tasks currently waiting for a semaphore permit  

These metrics will make it easier to diagnose whether copr latency is caused by scheduling pressure, semaphore contention, or other factors.
tikv/tikv#19179.title: Improve observability for coprocessor suspend time and semaphore contention

tikv/tikv#19180.body:
## Bug Report

https://github.com/tikv/tikv/blob/04df27b806a8801d3c94ba45dc9b54138d1c2d78/components/raftstore/src/store/fsm/peer.rs#L2262-L2275

After a peer becoming leader, it will report a heartbeat to pd instantly. But it just reuse the old approximate_size/keys stats. 

When there are many leader transfer operations in a short time, e.g. evict leader, these outdates stats can cause abrupt store size change in pd and trigges unnecessary region balance.
tikv/tikv#19180.title: Region approximate_size/keys may be incorrect after leader transfer

tikv/tikv#19201.body:
## Bug Report

<!-- Thanks for your bug report! Don't worry if you can't fill out all the sections. -->

In one production env, 1 tikv's read pool pending increased to the limit in a very short time and caused it reject all following read requests. And these pending tasks are all in waiting for snapshot state and never finish. This happened when this store's disk usage increased to NearFull state(due to a big compaction in rocksdb). 

I found the snapshot pending was due to follower read. Because when disk is near full, raft append is stopped and all follower on this store's raft applied index will fall behind. So the async_snapshot requests with readIndex can never return because raft apply is stopped while leader's committed index is still increasing. 

So in this case, I think when propose readIndex request on follower peer, if current store's disk is full, it should directly return an errror to the caller to let the caller try other replica instead of waiting forever.
tikv/tikv#19201.title: follower read request can be stuck forever when disk is full

tikv/tikv#19285.body:
## Bug Report

### What happened

Memory usage in TiKV grows unboundedly over time, particularly in the `concurrency_manager` LockTable. After leader eviction (QPS drops to 0), memory usage remains high and does not decrease.

### What did you expect to happen

Memory should be reclaimed after locks are released and GC runs.

### Root Cause

The bug is in `crossbeam-skiplist`'s `RefRange` iterator (`base.rs`).

`RefRange::next()` and `next_back()` use `clone_from()` to update `self.head`/`self.tail`:

```rust
self.head.clone_from(&next_head);
```

Since `RefEntry` has no `Drop` implementation (by design - callers must explicitly call `release()`), the old entry is dropped without decrementing its refcount. This causes permanent memory leaks.

The correct pattern (used in `RefIter::next()`) is:

```rust
if let Some(e) = mem::replace(&mut self.head, next_head.clone()) {
    unsafe { e.node.decrement(guard); }
}
```

### Impact

Any code using `SkipMap::range()` iterators leaks memory. In TiKV, this affects:
- `LockTable::check_range()`
- `LockTable::find_first()`

### Reproduction

Stress test results (10 seconds, range iteration + insert/remove cycle):
```
before fix:
t=10s ops=11786 len=7530 alloc=3MB
after fix:
t=10s ops=11710 len=3422 alloc=613MB
```
### Versions

- v6.5.6 <= version <= v8.5
tikv/tikv#19285.title: Memory leak in LockTable due to crossbeam-skiplist RefRange iterator bug

## Bug Report

See https://github.com/pingcap/tidb/issues/65299.
In `to_packed_u64`, when the value is of type TIMESTAMP, it is first converted to UTC.
However, the `Time::try_from_chrono_datetime` performs `TimeArgs::check`, which uses `ctx.time_zone`.
This applies the timezone offset again, and may incorrectly mark a valid timestamp as invalid,
especially near the lower bound (e.g. around 1970-01-01).

tikv/tikv#19392.body:
## Development Task
Previous work in https://github.com/tikv/tikv/pull/17029 introduced a mechanism in v8.3.0+ to wait to gain a larger batch until reaching either size or timeout. It introduces an adaptive batching strategy controlled by two key parameters: wait_duration (default: 20us) and batch_size_hint (default: 8KB). It dynamically adjusts the wait_duration to maintain an optimal balance, constraining it strictly between 0.5x and 2.0x of the configured value according to the observed batch trend. The problem with the current strategy is that when the wait_duration is configured to a large value, performance regression and latency increase may occur due to the limited range of variation.
We need a new adaptive batching strategy which can adjusts the wait_duration within a larger range to optimize the trade-off between write batching efficiency (reducing IOPS) and QPS throughput/request latency.
tikv/tikv#19392.title: Raftstore: support new adaptive batching strategy

tikv/tikv#19406.body:
## Development Task

When PD is temporarily unavailable during a log backup flush, `flush_ts` falls back to zero and `path_to_meta` synthesises a local timestamp with a random UUID suffix (`-SYNTHETIC...`). The synthetic timestamp is derived from `physical_now()` and bears no relation to earlier PD-sourced values, breaking the chronological ordering that `StreamMetaStorage::iter_prefix` relies on for **deterministic compaction** and **RPO calculation for CRR replication**.

**Expected behaviour:** 

Meta path format should be a deterministic, parseable layout:

- Old: `v1/backupmeta/{flush_ts}-{min_begin_ts}-{min_ts}-{max_ts}[-SYNTHETIC...].meta`
- New: `v1/backupmeta/{flush_ts:016X}{store_id:016X}-d{min_begin_ts:016X}l{min_ts:016X}u{max_ts:016X}.meta`

Where `flush_ts` is monotonically increasing.

Motivation: make every meta have a deterministic ID while preserving the original ordering behavior as much as possible.
tikv/tikv#19406.title: backup-stream: ensure monotonically increasing flush_ts per store

Observe result is empty. 4. Change only the name matcher to `name=~"unified.*" (or name=~"unified_read.*")`, then data appears. ### What did you expect?
